headline: Semis Surge to the Front as Oil Shock and Healthcare Drag Divide the Tape
body_html:
Midway through Tuesday's session, the market is holding a constructive posture, with advancers outnumbering decliners by roughly 220 to 171 across the Jaleel Capital universe. The dominant theme is a tale of two tapes: semiconductor names are powering ahead — the sector is up nearly 3% — while healthcare is the day's clear laggard, off more than 1.5%, as a cluster of medical-device names absorbs significant selling pressure.

The standout mover is Semtech, surging nearly 9% to lead all gainers, with Monolithic Power and Sandisk adding further conviction to the chip complex's advance. CrowdStrike and Okta are also commanding attention, both up more than 7–8%, suggesting that despite a modest drag in the broader software sector, security-oriented names are catching genuine bids. On the other side, Biogen is off more than 7%, and three healthcare-device companies — GE HealthCare, Stryker, and Globus Medical — are each down roughly 4–6%, amplifying the sector's weakness. S&P 500 futures turned positive after the June inflation print crossed the wire, adding a macro tailwind to the session.

Into the close, traders should watch whether the semiconductor rally can sustain its breadth or whether profit-taking narrows the advance. Escalating tensions in the Strait of Hormuz are keeping energy markets restless and could inject volatility into the final hour if fresh headlines emerge.

briefs.headline: June Inflation Data Lifts Futures Off the Mat
briefs.body: S&P 500 futures shifted into positive territory after the release of June consumer-price figures, providing the session's clearest macro catalyst. The move helped stabilize broader sentiment and gave rate-sensitive growth names a modest lift heading into the afternoon.

briefs.headline: Strait of Hormuz Standoff Drives Oil to Four-Week Peak
briefs.body: Crude prices climbed to their highest level in roughly a month as the United States and Iran exchanged fresh strikes and both sides battled for influence over the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz. The confrontation has unsettled tanker routes and stoked supply-disruption fears across Gulf markets, most of which fell on the escalating hostilities.

briefs.headline: Bloom Energy Catches a Bid Amid Middle East Energy Jitters
briefs.body: Bloom Energy rose more than 6%, making it one of the session's brightest spots in the energy complex. Traders appear to be rotating toward domestic alternative-energy providers as geopolitical risk around conventional oil supply chains intensifies, giving fuel-cell plays an indirect tailwind.

briefs.headline: Healthcare Devices Under Pressure as Sector Sells Off Broadly
briefs.body: Stryker, GE HealthCare, and Globus Medical are each nursing losses of between 4.5% and 5.6%, dragging the healthcare sector down more than 1.5% — the worst showing of any group on the day. The synchronized decline points to sector-wide repositioning rather than company-specific news flow.

briefs.headline: Retail Traders Eye AI Supply-Chain Options as SK Hynix Interest Builds
briefs.body: Momentum-oriented retail participants, dubbed 'bottleneck bros' in market circles, are reportedly focused on the AI hardware supply chain and are watching for options access tied to memory chipmaker SK Hynix. The interest underscores how the AI infrastructure trade continues to capture speculative imagination even on volatile macro days.

briefs.headline: China's Oil Appetite Slips to Near-Decade Low as Iran Conflict Reshapes Import Flows
briefs.body: China's crude imports for June fell to their lowest level in nearly ten years, complicating the market's read on how sustained Middle East conflict will affect global demand. Analysts are debating whether Beijing's prior stockpiling gives it a buffer against the current supply shock or whether a prolonged Hormuz disruption would eventually force import behavior to change.
